excerpts: With agent teams, multiple Claude instances work in parallel on a shared codebase without active human intervention.

y implementation of parallel Claude is bare-bones. A new bare git repo is created, and for each agent, a Docker container is spun up with the repo mounted to `/upstream` . Each agent clones a local copy to `/workspace` , and when it's done, pushes from its own local container to upstream

Claude takes a "lock" on a task by writing a text file to current_tasks/ (e.g., one agent might lock current_tasks/parse_if_statement.txt, while another locks current_tasks/codegen_function_definition.txt). If two agents try to claim the same task, git's synchronization forces the second agent to pick a different one.

This is a very early research prototype. I haven’t yet implemented any other method for communication between agents, nor do I enforce any process for managing high-level goals. I don’t use an orchestration agent.

Parallelism also enables specialization. LLM-written code frequently re-implements existing functionality, so I tasked one agent with coalescing any duplicate code it found. I put another in charge of improving the performance of the compiler itself, and a third I made responsible for outputting efficient compiled code.

Running multiple instances in parallel can address two weaknesses of a single-agent harness:
One Claude Code session can only do one thing at a time. Especially as the scope of a project expands, debugging multiple issues in parallel is far more efficient.
Running multiple Claude agents allows for specialization.
Merge conflicts are frequent, but Claude is smart enough to figure that out.
I haven’t yet implemented any other method for communication between agents, nor do I enforce any process for managing high-level goals.

excerpts: Abstract: Software development is a complex, multi-phase process traditionally requiring collaboration among individuals with diverse expertise. We propose AgentMesh, a Python-based framework that uses multiple cooperating LLM-powered agents to automate software development tasks.

The Planner agent first decomposes user requests into concrete subtasks; the Coder agent implements each subtask in code; the Debugger agent tests and fixes the code; and the Reviewer agent validates the final output for correctness and quality.
A case study illustrates AgentMesh handling a non-trivial development request via sequential task planning, code generation, iterative debugging, and final code review.
We describe the architecture and design of these agents and their communication, and provide implementation details including prompt strategies and workflow orchestration.

excerpts: The [OpenAI Agents SDK](https://github.com/openai/openai-agents-python) enables you to build agentic AI apps in a lightweight, easy-to-use package with very few abstractions.
It's a production-ready upgrade of our previous experimentation for agents, [Swarm
The Agents SDK has a very small set of primitives:
**Agents** , which are LLMs equipped with instructions and tools
**Agents as tools / Handoffs** , which allow agents to delegate to other agents for specific tasks
**Guardrails** , which enable validation of agent inputs and outputs
In addition, the SDK comes with built-in **tracing** that lets you visualize and debug your agentic flows, as well as evaluate them and even fine-tune models for your application.
**Agent loop** : A built-in agent loop that handles tool invocation, sends results back to the LLM, and continues until the task is complete.
**Agents as tools / Handoffs** : A powerful mechanism for coordinating and delegating work across multiple agents.
**Guardrails** : Run input validation and safety checks in parallel with agent execution, and fail fast when checks do not pass.
**Sessions** : A persistent memory layer for maintaining working context within an agent loop.
**Tracing** : Built-in tracing for visualizing, debugging, and monitoring workflows, with support for the OpenAI suite of evaluation, fine-tuning, and distillation tools.

excerpts: Abstract: Software development is a complex, multi-phase process traditionally requiring collaboration among individuals with diverse expertise. We propose AgentMesh, a Python-based framework that uses multiple cooperating LLM-powered agents to automate software development tasks.

The Planner agent first decomposes user requests into concrete subtasks; the Coder agent implements each subtask in code; the Debugger agent tests and fixes the code; and the Reviewer agent validates the final output for correctness and quality.
A case study illustrates AgentMesh handling a non-trivial development request via sequential task planning, code generation, iterative debugging, and final code review.
We describe the architecture and design of these agents and their communication, and provide implementation details including prompt strategies and workflow orchestration.

Finally, we examine current limitations - such as error propagation and context scaling - and outline future work toward more robust, scalable multi-agent AI systems for software engineering automation.

excerpts: LangGraph is a library built on top of LangChain that focuses on **explicit control flow** for LLM-based applications.
Instead of structuring an application as a linear pipeline, LangGraph models it as a graph where execution can branch, loop, and revisit earlier steps.

LangGraph addresses this by allowing **cycles** in the graph. Nodes can be revisited, decisions can be made repeatedly, and control flow is handled explicitly rather than being embedded inside prompts.
At a conceptual level, LangGraph is built around a small set of core abstractions. An agent is defined as a graph in which all steps share and update a common state.

excerpts: Section Title: Building LangGraph: Designing an Agent Runtime from first principles
In this blog piece, you’ll learn why and how we built LangGraph for production agents—focusing on control, durability, and the core features needed to scale.
ummary:** We launched LangGraph as a low level agent framework nearly two years ago, and have already seen companies like LinkedIn, Uber, and Klarna use it to build production ready agents
LangGraph builds upon feedback from the super popular LangChain framework, and rethinks how agent frameworks should work for production.
We aimed to find the right abstraction for AI agents, and decided that was little to no abstraction at all. Instead, we focused on control and durability.

Running multiple instances in parallel can address two weaknesses of a single-agent harness:
One Claude Code session can only do one thing at a time. Especially as the scope of a project expands, debugging multiple issues in parallel is far more efficient.
Running multiple Claude agents allows for specialization.
Merge conflicts are frequent, but Claude is smart enough to figure that out.

Parallelism also enables specialization. LLM-written code frequently re-implements existing functionality, so I tasked one agent with coalescing any duplicate code it found. y implementation of parallel Claude is bare-bones. A new bare git repo is created, and for each agent, a Docker container is spun up with the repo mounted to `/upstream` . Each agent clones a local copy to `/workspace` , and when it's done, pushes from its own local container to upstream

excerpts: Abstract: Software development is a complex, multi-phase process traditionally requiring collaboration among individuals with diverse expertise. We propose AgentMesh, a Python-based framework that uses multiple cooperating LLM-powered agents to automate software development tasks.

The Planner agent first decomposes user requests into concrete subtasks; the Coder agent implements each subtask in code; the Debugger agent tests and fixes the code; and the Reviewer agent validates the final output for correctness and quality.
A case study illustrates AgentMesh handling a non-trivial development request via sequential task planning, code generation, iterative debugging, and final code review.
We describe the architecture and design of these agents and their communication, and provide implementation details including prompt strategies and workflow orchestration.

Finally, we examine current limitations - such as error propagation and context scaling - and outline future work toward more robust, scalable multi-agent AI systems for software engineering automation.

excerpts: **Tracing** : Built-in tracing for visualizing, debugging, and monitoring workflows, with support for the OpenAI suite of evaluation, fine-tuning, and distillation tools.
The [OpenAI Agents SDK](https://github.com/openai/openai-agents-python) enables you to build agentic AI apps in a lightweight, easy-to-use package with very few abstractions.
It's a production-ready upgrade of our previous experimentation for agents, [Swarm
The Agents SDK has a very small set of primitives:
**Agents** , which are LLMs equipped with instructions and tools
**Agents as tools / Handoffs** , which allow agents to delegate to other agents for specific tasks
**Guardrails** , which enable validation of agent inputs and outputs
In addition, the SDK comes with built-in **tracing** that lets you visualize and debug your agentic flows, as well as evaluate them and even fine-tune models for your application.

excerpts: Abstract: Software development is a complex, multi-phase process traditionally requiring collaboration among individuals with diverse expertise. We propose AgentMesh, a Python-based framework that uses multiple cooperating LLM-powered agents to automate software development tasks.

The Planner agent first decomposes user requests into concrete subtasks; the Coder agent implements each subtask in code; the Debugger agent tests and fixes the code; and the Reviewer agent validates the final output for correctness and quality.
A case study illustrates AgentMesh handling a non-trivial development request via sequential task planning, code generation, iterative debugging, and final code review.

If two agents try to claim the same task, git's synchronization forces the second agent to pick a different one.

This is a very early research prototype. I haven’t yet implemented any other method for communication between agents, nor do I enforce any process for managing high-level goals. I don’t use an orchestration agent.
Merge conflicts are frequent, but Claude is smart enough to figure that out.

excerpts: Abstract: Software development is a complex, multi-phase process traditionally requiring collaboration among individuals with diverse expertise. We propose AgentMesh, a Python-based framework that uses multiple cooperating LLM-powered agents to automate software development tasks.

The Planner agent first decomposes user requests into concrete subtasks; the Coder agent implements each subtask in code; the Debugger agent tests and fixes the code; and the Reviewer agent validates the final output for correctness and quality.
A case study illustrates AgentMesh handling a non-trivial development request via sequential task planning, code generation, iterative debugging, and final code review.
We describe the architecture and design of these agents and their communication, and provide implementation details including prompt strategies and workflow orchestration.

Finally, we examine current limitations - such as error propagation and context scaling - and outline future work toward more robust, scalable multi-agent AI systems for software engineering automation.

excerpts: Abstract: Software development is a complex, multi-phase process traditionally requiring collaboration among individuals with diverse expertise. We propose AgentMesh, a Python-based framework that uses multiple cooperating LLM-powered agents to automate software development tasks.

The Planner agent first decomposes user requests into concrete subtasks; the Coder agent implements each subtask in code; the Debugger agent tests and fixes the code; and the Reviewer agent validates the final output for correctness and quality.
A case study illustrates AgentMesh handling a non-trivial development request via sequential task planning, code generation, iterative debugging, and final code review.
We describe the architecture and design of these agents and their communication, and provide implementation details including prompt strategies and workflow orchestration.

executive_summary.description: A high-level overview of the state of agentic AI systems for 2025-2026, summarizing the key trends in multi-agent composition patterns, orchestration frameworks, and production-level challenges.

pattern_name.description: The name of the emerging composition pattern (e.g., Agent Mesh, Hierarchical Planning).

description.description: A brief description of how the pattern works.

ideal_use_case.description: The type of problem or scenario where this pattern is most effective.

key_emerging_composition_patterns.description: A list of the most significant new multi-agent composition patterns that have emerged beyond traditional ones like chain and fan-out.

pattern_name.description: The name of the advanced pattern being analyzed (e.g., Agent Meshes, Agent Markets/Auctions).

mechanism.description: A detailed explanation of the pattern's underlying mechanism and how agents interact.

use_cases.description: Specific examples of applications or problems where this pattern is suitable.

trade_offs.description: The advantages and disadvantages of using this pattern, such as complexity, efficiency, or robustness.

context_transfer.description: Methods and strategies for passing context and state between agents in a workflow.

result_routing.description: Techniques for routing the output of one agent to the correct subsequent agent or process.

agent_lifecycle_management.description: How systems manage the creation, execution, suspension, and termination of agents.

inter_agent_communication.description: Protocols and communication styles used for interaction between agents (e.g., message passing, state machine execution).

framework_name.description: The name of the multi-agent framework.

topology.description: The communication topology supported by the framework (e.g., Star, Mesh, Graph, Chain).

role_structure.description: How agent roles are defined and managed (e.g., Static, Dynamic, Symmetric).

communication_style.description: The primary method of communication between agents (e.g., Message Passing, State Machine, SOP Documents).

conflict_resolution.description: The mechanism for resolving disagreements or conflicting outputs from agents (e.g., Human in the loop, Verifier Agent, Voting).

core_primitives.description: The fundamental building blocks of the SDK, such as Agents, Handoffs, and Guardrails.

key_features.description: Notable features including the built-in agent loop, session management for memory, and tracing for observability.

multi_agent_orchestration.description: How the SDK facilitates multi-agent workflows, particularly through the 'Handoffs' mechanism.

evolution_from_swarm.description: The relationship to the earlier experimental 'Swarm' library and the improvements made in the official SDK.

core_concept.description: The central idea of Anthropic's approach, such as 'Agent Teams' or parallel Claude instances.

execution_model.description: The model for how multiple agents work together, such as parallel execution with a shared codebase and git-based synchronization.

agent_specialization.description: The use of specialized agents with distinct roles (e.g., Coder, Debugger, Reviewer, Documentation writer).

community_ecosystem.description: Information on the community-driven ecosystem of pre-built sub-agents and orchestration recipes.

design_philosophy.description: The core principle behind LangGraph, such as enabling explicit, cyclical control flow for building reliable agents.

core_abstractions.description: The main components of a LangGraph application, specifically the shared State, Nodes (workers), and Edges (control flow).

persistence.description: How LangGraph manages durability and statefulness, particularly through the use of checkpoints.

multi_agent_patterns.description: Common multi-agent architectures built with LangGraph, such as agent supervisors, routers, and handoffs.

core_concepts.description: The fundamental building blocks of CrewAI: role-based Agents, Tasks, and Crews.

process_management.description: How workflows are orchestrated, including support for sequential and hierarchical processes.

recent_advancements.description: Key features introduced in recent versions (2025-2026), such as async support, multimodal capabilities, and A2A (Agent-to-Agent) task execution utilities.

memory_and_context.description: Details on how CrewAI handles short-term and long-term memory for agents within a crew.

architecture_overview.description: Description of the major redesign in AutoGen v0.4, focusing on its asynchronous, event-driven, and layered architecture.

api_layers.description: Explanation of the different layers, including the foundational Core API and the high-level AgentChat API.

collaboration_patterns.description: How multi-agent conversations are managed, such as through GroupChat, RoundRobinGroupChat, and other team interfaces.

developer_tooling.description: Information on tools within the ecosystem, like AutoGen Studio for rapid prototyping and AutoGen Bench for evaluation.

pattern_name.description: The name of the AWS architectural pattern (e.g., Amazon Bedrock AgentCore, Agent Squad).

description.description: A summary of the pattern's approach to multi-agent orchestration.

core_aws_services.description: The primary AWS services used to implement this pattern (e.g., Amazon Bedrock, AWS Step Functions, AWS Lambda).

name.description: The name of the framework or protocol (e.g., Google A2A, Microsoft Semantic Kernel).

description.description: A brief summary of the technology and its purpose in the context of agentic systems.

relevance.description: How this technology contributes to multi-agent composition or orchestration.

root_cause.description: The underlying reason for the failure mode.
